title: Lung Cancer — NSCLC
oneLiner: Most common lung cancer (85%); insidious onset in smokers >50; often advanced at diagnosis leading to poor survival.

sections.Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: **One-liner:** Most common lung cancer (85%); insidious onset in smokers >50; often advanced at diagnosis leading to poor survival.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:** A 67-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents with a persistent cough for 3 months, not responding to antibiotics. He notices mild hemoptysis (streaks) and has lost 4 kg unintentionally. He has a dull ache in the right chest. On examination, he is afebrile, with reduced breath sounds at the right upper zone and finger clubbing.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Age >60, heavy smoker:** Strongest risk factor – cumulative carcinogen exposure leads to field cancerisation.
- **Chronic cough >8 weeks:** Needs imaging – bronchial irritation from tumour.
- **Hemoptysis:** Tumour eroding into airway vascular submucosa.
- **Weight loss:** Systemic catabolism from tumour cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6) or paraneoplastic syndromes.
- **Localised chest ache:** Pleural or chest wall invasion – consider Pancoast tumour if apical.
- **Reduced breath sounds +/- dullness:** Consolidation or collapse from endobronchial obstruction; may indicate post-obstructive pneumonia.
- **Finger clubbing:** Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (HPOA) – seen in 5-10% of NSCLC (especially adenocarcinoma and squamous cell).

**Atypical presentations (watch for traps):**
1. **Pancoast syndrome (superior sulcus tumour):** Shoulder/arm pain, Horner’s (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), ulnar neuropathy – not cough.
2. **Hoarseness (left recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy):** No respiratory symptoms – just voice change, from mediastinal nodal involvement.
3. **Paraneoplastic syndromes:** Hypercalcaemia (squamous cell – PTHrP), hypertrophic osteoarthropathy (adenocarcinoma), clubbing, gynaecomastia (large cell), neuromyopathies (Lambert-Eaton, sensory neuropathy) – these may present before lung symptoms.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** NSCLC is a malignant epithelial tumour arising from the bronchi, bronchioles, or alveoli. It comprises three main histotypes – adenocarcinoma (~40%), squamous cell carcinoma (~30%), large cell carcinoma (~10%) – plus rare subtypes. It is a heterogeneous disease, both in growth pattern and molecular drivers.

**Why this matters:**
- **Frequency:** NSCLC accounts for 85% of all lung cancers. Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer death globally.
- **Stakes:** Most patients are diagnosed at stage III or IV (unresectable). Five-year survival for metastatic NSCLC is ~7% – but early stage (IA) can be cured (survival >80% with surgery).
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking lung cancer for pneumonia (especially in smokers) delays diagnosis; forgetting to subtype and test for driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1) denies patients targeted therapy or immunotherapy.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**
1. **Initiation:** Chronic exposure to carcinogens (tobacco smoke, radon, asbestos) causes DNA damage – mutations in tumour suppressor genes (TP53, RB1) and oncogenes (KRAS, EGFR, ALK, BRAF). Smokers have a high mutational burden.
2. **Promotion:** Sustained inflammation and epigenetic changes allow damaged cells to proliferate. Airway epithelium undergoes metaplasia → dysplasia → carcinoma in situ → invasive cancer.
3. **Progression:** As tumour grows, it invades bronchial walls, obstructs airways, erodes blood vessels, and spreads to lymph nodes and distant organs.

**Pathophysiology → Clinical Signs:**
- **Cough ± infection:** Tumour mass irritates cough receptors and obstructs bronchi, causing post-obstructive pneumonia.
- **Hemoptysis:** Erosion of bronchial arteries (high pressure) – the bright red streaking is classic for airway source.
- **Chest pain:** Involvement of visceral pleura (dull ache) or parietal pleura/chest wall (sharp, pleuritic).
- **Clubbing ± HPOA:** Paraneoplastic release of VEGF and PDGF stimulates periosteal new bone formation – you’ll see periositis on X-ray.
- **Wheeze (localised):** Large airway compression – not diffuse like asthma.
- **Superior vena cava syndrome (SVC):** Right-sided tumours compress SVC → facial oedema, plethora, distended neck veins.
- **Horner’s (Pancoast):** Sympathetic chain invasion causing ipsilateral ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis.

**Molecular patterns (why testing matters):**
- **Adenocarcinoma:** Often peripheral, associated with EGFR mutations (non-smokers, Asian, female), ALK rearrangements (younger, never/light smokers), and KRAS mutations (smokers, poor prognosis).
- **Squamous cell:** Central, cavitating, high PD-L1 expression (immunotherapy response).
- **Large cell:** Rapid growth, peripheral, usually non-squamous non-adenocarcinoma histology.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Strongest risk** | Cigarette smoking (accounts for 80-90% of cases) | Heavily smoking history (>30 pack-years) |
| **Occupational** | Asbestos (synergistic with smoking), radon (uranium miners), arsenic, nickel, chromium | History of mining, construction, shipbuilding |
| **Environmental** | Radon gas (second leading cause in non-smokers), air pollution (PM2.5) | Non-smoker with home in high-radon area or urban pollution |
| **Genetic/ familial** | First-degree relative with lung cancer (especially in never-smokers) | Young onset or non-smoker with family history |
| **Medical** | Chronic lung disease (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis – increased risk) | Patient already under respiratory follow-up |
| **Infectious** | Not a direct cause – but HIV (weakened immunity) increases risk | Immunosuppressed patient |
| **Other** | Prior thoracic radiotherapy (e.g., for breast cancer, lymphoma) | History of chest radiation |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking – the dose-response is linear. Even second-hand smoke increases risk.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Histological confirmation from biopsy (CT-guided, bronchoscopic, EBUS-TBNA, or surgical) + staging imaging (PET-CT, MRI brain). Staging uses **TNM (8th edition)** – not detailed here, but crucial for management.

**First test for suspicion:**
- **Chest X-ray** – shows mass, effusion, collapse, hilar prominence. *However, normal CXR does not exclude cancer.*

**Critical work-up:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation trap |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **CT chest + upper abdomen** | Tumour size, location, invasion, mediastinal lymph nodes, liver/adrenal metastases | Definitive staging scan; needed for TNM | Don’t confuse atelectasis for tumour |
| **PET-CT** | Metabolic activity (SUV) | Identifies viable tumour vs scar; detects distant metastases | False positives in infection/inflammation |
| **MRI brain** | Brain metastases (lung cancer is #1 source of brain mets) | Mandatory for stage III–IV; consider for all NSCLC | Small nodules may be missed without contrast |
| **Bronchoscopy with biopsy** | Endobronchial tumour, washings, brushings | Central tumours; also for haemoptysis | Negative biopsy does not rule out – need peripheral needle |
| **CT-guided lung biopsy** | Peripheral nodule core | Diagnostic yield >90% | Pneumothorax risk (15-30%) |
| **EBUS-TBNA** | Mediastinal/hilar nodes | Nodal staging – crucial for deciding surgery vs chemoRT | False negatives in small nodes <5 mm |
| **Sputum cytology** | Malignant cells | Only useful for central, squamous or small cell – poor sensitivity | High false negative rate |

**Single finding that confirms:** Histopathology report showing malignant epithelial tumour with histological subtyping (adenocarcinoma, squamous, large cell) and molecular markers (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1). *You need both histology and molecular profile.*

**Pattern-recognition clue for subtype:** Cavitating central mass on CT = squamous cell; ground-glass nodule = lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma (formerly BAC).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
*Management is stage-driven – use multidisciplinary team (MDT) decision.*

**Immediate (first hour – after diagnosis):**
- **Stabilise if symptomatic:** Oxygen if hypoxic; analgesia for chest pain (avoid NSAIDs if renal impaired); treat post-obstructive pneumonia with antibiotics (co-amoxiclav IV).
- **Call respiratory oncology team** – don’t start chemotherapy without full staging.
- **If SVC syndrome:** Head-up position, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, urgent oncology review for radiotherapy/stenting.

**Ward (24-72 h – staging and planning):**
- **Confirm histological subtype and molecular profile.** Await EGFR/ALK/ROS1/PD-L1 results (5-10 days). Do NOT start empiric chemo before results – you may miss targeted therapy.
- **PET-CT and MRI brain** within 48 h if not done.
- **Pulmonary function tests** for surgical fitness (FEV1, DLCO) if early stage.
- **Consider VTE prophylaxis** (lung cancer + hypercoagulable) – enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless contraindicated.

**Long-term (MDT-defined plan):**

| Stage | Treatment | Why |
|-------|-----------|-----|
| **IA–IIB (T1-2 N0 M0)** | Lobectomy + mediastinal lymph node dissection (or segmentectomy for small GGO lesions) | Potentially curative; 5-yr survival 80-40% depending on stage |
| **IIIA (N2 positive)** | Neoadjuvant chemoRT (cisplatin + etoposide) then surgery, or definitive chemoRT | N2 disease requires combined modality |
| **IIIB–IIIC (unresectable)** | Definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy: cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1 + etoposide 100 mg/m² IV days 1-3, every 21 days, with RT 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions; then durvalumab (PD-L1 inhibitor) consolidation for 12 months | Durvalumab (PACIFIC regimen) improves PFS and OS |
| **IV (metastatic)** | If driver mutation +ve: targeted TKI (osimertinib for EGFR; alectinib for ALK; entrectinib for ROS1). If no driver: first-line chemo-immunotherapy (pembrolizumab + carboplatin + pemetrexed for non-squamous; for squamous: pembrolizumab + carboplatin + nab-paclitaxel). Cisplatin/pemetrexed also used. | Molecular testing guides first move |

**Drug doses (inline):**
- **Osimertinib** 80 mg PO once daily (first line EGFR mutant).
- **Alectinib** 600 mg PO twice daily (ALK rearrangement).
- **Pembrolizumab** 200 mg IV q3w (PD-L1 >50% monotherapy; or + chemotherapy if PD-L1 1-49%).
- **Carboplatin AUC5-6 IV q3w + pemetrexed** 500 mg/m² IV q3w (non-squamous).
- **Cisplatin** 75 mg/m² IV q3w.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ Don't give anti-EGFR TKI without EGFR mutation – will cause toxicity with no benefit.
- ❌ Don't start steroids for brain metastases before biopsy – may interfere with histology.
- ❌ Don't give prophylactic cranial irradiation in NSCLC (unlike SCLC).
- ❌ Don't offer surgery for N3 or M1 disease – it worsens survival.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | NSCLC | Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) | Pulmonary TB | Benign Lung Nodule (e.g., hamartoma) |
|---------|-------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache (chest wall invasion) | Usually none unless metastatic | Pleuritic chest pain if effusion | Usually none |
| **Exam** | Clubbing, reduced breath sounds | No clubbing; often SVC syndrome | Crepitations, may have tachypnoea | Normal |
| **CXR/CT** | Solitary nodule or mass; spiculated borders, cavitation (squamous), ground glass (adenocarcinoma) | Central mass, bulky mediastinal nodes, often early effusion | Apical infiltrates, cavitation (ghoul lesions), hilar nodes | Well-defined, round, sometimes “popcorn” calcification (cartilage) |
| **Labs** | Normal (unless paraneoplastic: hyperCa, ↑ALP) | Normal; can have SIADH, ACTH (Cushing’s) | Positive Quantiferon/IGRA, sputum AFB (if cavitary) | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Histology + molecular markers | Biopsy shows small cells with miliary necrosis; rapid doubling time (<30 days) | TB contacts, night sweats, fevers; positive culture/NAT | Stable on repeat CT (2 yr), non-FDG avid on PET |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
1. **Post-obstructive pneumonia:** Recurrent chest infections, slow resolution → lung abscess (foul sputum, high fever). **What to do:** CT chest + IV antibiotics (piperacillin/tazobactam), consider bronchoscopy for drainage.
2. **Malignant pleural effusion:** Dyspnoea, dullness, stony dull percussion. **Signs:** Shift of trachea away (if large). **What to do:** Ultrasound-guided thoracentesis (send for cytology, pH, glucose). Offer pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter.
3. **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO):** Facial fullness, distended neck veins, cyanosis, stridor. **Emergency.** **What to do:** Head-up, IV dexamethasone, repeat CT with contrast, urgent radiotherapy (8 Gy in 1 fraction for palliation) or SVC stent.
4. **Brain metastases:** Headache, nausea, seizures, focal deficits. **Signs:** Papilloedema on fundoscopy. **What to do:** MRI brain; IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat then 4 mg QDS; whole-brain RT or stereotactic radiosurgery if oligometastatic.
5. **Hypercalcaemia (squamous):** Lethargy, constipation, polyuria, confusion. ECG short QT. **What to do:** IV fluids 0.9% saline 1L over 1h then 3L/day, bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg IV). Check for PTHrP.
6. **Lung abscess (cavitating squamous):** High fever, productive cough, haemoptysis. CT shows cavity with air-fluid level. **What to do:** IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav + metronidazole), or percutaneous drainage if >3 cm.
7. **Pulmonary metastasis to the opposite lung:** New nodules on follow-up CT. This upsages to M1a.

**Late-presentation trap:** A patient with weight loss, back pain (vertebral metastasis), and leg weakness – may be misdiagnosed as spinal disc disease. *Always consider bone mets in a smoker with back pain.*
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **BEST Mnemonic for NSCLC workup:**  
  B – **Biopsy** (histology + molecular)  
  E – **Excision** (surgery if stage I-II)  
  S – **Staging** (PET-CT + MRI brain)  
  T – **Targeted therapy** (if driver mutation)

- **SQUAMOUS clues:**  
  S – Smoking, Sulcus (cavitation)  
  Q – Quit? No, they’re still smoking  
  U – Ugh, hypercalcaemia (PTHrP)  
  A – Atelectasis (central)  
  M – Mass in mediastinum (if big)  
  O – Oncology biopsy (PD-L1 high)  
  U – Ulceration? No  
  S – Spiculated margins

- **PARANEOPLASTIC (NSCLC specific):**  
  H – Hypercalcaemia (squamous)  
  O – Osteoarthropathy (HPOA, clubbing)  
  P – Peripheral neuropathy / LEMS  
  E – Endocrine (gynaecomastia – large cell)

**High-yield pearls:**

1. **Never clear a smoker’s “chest infection” without an X-ray** – if cough persists >6 weeks, CT is mandatory.
2. **Adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer in never-smokers and women.** Always test EGFR, ALK, ROS1 even if non-smoker.
3. **Clubbing + haemoptysis + weight loss = lung cancer until proven otherwise.**
4. **A single pulmonary nodule in a smoker >50 years old is malignant until proven otherwise** – need FDG-PET or biopsy.
5. **If you see a cavitating lesion in a smoker, think squamous cell carcinoma** – not always TB.
6. **Do not order a CT-guided biopsy for a small (5-10 mm) ground-glass nodule** – consider surveillance CT in 3 months (risk of pneumothorax outweighs benefit).
7. **When a patient with known NSCLC develops worsening dyspnoea, think malignant pericardial effusion, lymphangitic carcinomatosis, or pulmonary embolism** – all need urgent echo/CTPA.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- ❌ Ordering only chest X-ray to follow up a nodule – need CT for growth assessment.
- ❌ Assuming a non-smoker cannot have lung cancer.
- ❌ Starting steroids before biopsy when brain mets are suspected – risks missing diagnosis if lymphoma/toxoplasmosis.
- ❌ Giving prophylactic PCV vaccine (Pneumovax) before splenectomy – not relevant here, but sometimes confused with risk of post-obstructive pneumonia.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Trigger | When to call | What to do |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| **SVCO (urgent)** | Stridor, severe facial oedema, cyanosis, inability to lie flat | Call oncology/radiology for urgent stent or radiotherapy; ICU if airway compromise |
| **Massive haemoptysis** (>300 ml/24h or any with airway compromise) | Crash team, respiratory reg, thoracic surgery | Intubate if GCS<8, place patient bleeding side down, rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade |
| **NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4** | Sepsis secondary to post-obstructive pneumonia | ICU; start IV antibiotics, consider bronchoscopy for clearance |
| **GCS <13 or new focal neurology** | Suspect brain metastases or leptomeningeal disease | Urgent CT head, start dexamethasone 8 mg IV stat, call oncology |
| **Hypercalcaemia** (corrected Ca >3.0 mmol/L) | Medical reg for IV fluids + bisphosphonate; if symptomatic (ECG changes, confusion), call ICU | Cardiac monitoring, IV bisphosphonate, consider calcitonin |
| **Pleural effusion causing respiratory distress** | Respiratory reg for ultrasound-guided drainage | Diagnostic tap first (send for pH, protein, LDH, cytology); therapeutic tap if >1L until dyspnoea relieved |

**General threshold for ICU referral:** Any patient with rising NEWS2, persistent hypoxia (SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂), or need for non-invasive ventilation. Lung cancer patients can be very frail and may decline escalation – always have the goals of care discussion early.

rawMarkdown:
## Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

**One-liner:** Most common lung cancer (85%); insidious onset in smokers >50; often advanced at diagnosis leading to poor survival.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:** A 67-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents with a persistent cough for 3 months, not responding to antibiotics. He notices mild hemoptysis (streaks) and has lost 4 kg unintentionally. He has a dull ache in the right chest. On examination, he is afebrile, with reduced breath sounds at the right upper zone and finger clubbing.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Age >60, heavy smoker:** Strongest risk factor – cumulative carcinogen exposure leads to field cancerisation.
- **Chronic cough >8 weeks:** Needs imaging – bronchial irritation from tumour.
- **Hemoptysis:** Tumour eroding into airway vascular submucosa.
- **Weight loss:** Systemic catabolism from tumour cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-6) or paraneoplastic syndromes.
- **Localised chest ache:** Pleural or chest wall invasion – consider Pancoast tumour if apical.
- **Reduced breath sounds +/- dullness:** Consolidation or collapse from endobronchial obstruction; may indicate post-obstructive pneumonia.
- **Finger clubbing:** Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (HPOA) – seen in 5-10% of NSCLC (especially adenocarcinoma and squamous cell).

**Atypical presentations (watch for traps):**
1. **Pancoast syndrome (superior sulcus tumour):** Shoulder/arm pain, Horner’s (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), ulnar neuropathy – not cough.
2. **Hoarseness (left recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy):** No respiratory symptoms – just voice change, from mediastinal nodal involvement.
3. **Paraneoplastic syndromes:** Hypercalcaemia (squamous cell – PTHrP), hypertrophic osteoarthropathy (adenocarcinoma), clubbing, gynaecomastia (large cell), neuromyopathies (Lambert-Eaton, sensory neuropathy) – these may present before lung symptoms.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** NSCLC is a malignant epithelial tumour arising from the bronchi, bronchioles, or alveoli. It comprises three main histotypes – adenocarcinoma (~40%), squamous cell carcinoma (~30%), large cell carcinoma (~10%) – plus rare subtypes. It is a heterogeneous disease, both in growth pattern and molecular drivers.

**Why this matters:**
- **Frequency:** NSCLC accounts for 85% of all lung cancers. Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer death globally.
- **Stakes:** Most patients are diagnosed at stage III or IV (unresectable). Five-year survival for metastatic NSCLC is ~7% – but early stage (IA) can be cured (survival >80% with surgery).
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking lung cancer for pneumonia (especially in smokers) delays diagnosis; forgetting to subtype and test for driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1) denies patients targeted therapy or immunotherapy.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**
1. **Initiation:** Chronic exposure to carcinogens (tobacco smoke, radon, asbestos) causes DNA damage – mutations in tumour suppressor genes (TP53, RB1) and oncogenes (KRAS, EGFR, ALK, BRAF). Smokers have a high mutational burden.
2. **Promotion:** Sustained inflammation and epigenetic changes allow damaged cells to proliferate. Airway epithelium undergoes metaplasia → dysplasia → carcinoma in situ → invasive cancer.
3. **Progression:** As tumour grows, it invades bronchial walls, obstructs airways, erodes blood vessels, and spreads to lymph nodes and distant organs.

**Pathophysiology → Clinical Signs:**
- **Cough ± infection:** Tumour mass irritates cough receptors and obstructs bronchi, causing post-obstructive pneumonia.
- **Hemoptysis:** Erosion of bronchial arteries (high pressure) – the bright red streaking is classic for airway source.
- **Chest pain:** Involvement of visceral pleura (dull ache) or parietal pleura/chest wall (sharp, pleuritic).
- **Clubbing ± HPOA:** Paraneoplastic release of VEGF and PDGF stimulates periosteal new bone formation – you’ll see periositis on X-ray.
- **Wheeze (localised):** Large airway compression – not diffuse like asthma.
- **Superior vena cava syndrome (SVC):** Right-sided tumours compress SVC → facial oedema, plethora, distended neck veins.
- **Horner’s (Pancoast):** Sympathetic chain invasion causing ipsilateral ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis.

**Molecular patterns (why testing matters):**
- **Adenocarcinoma:** Often peripheral, associated with EGFR mutations (non-smokers, Asian, female), ALK rearrangements (younger, never/light smokers), and KRAS mutations (smokers, poor prognosis).
- **Squamous cell:** Central, cavitating, high PD-L1 expression (immunotherapy response).
- **Large cell:** Rapid growth, peripheral, usually non-squamous non-adenocarcinoma histology.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Strongest risk** | Cigarette smoking (accounts for 80-90% of cases) | Heavily smoking history (>30 pack-years) |
| **Occupational** | Asbestos (synergistic with smoking), radon (uranium miners), arsenic, nickel, chromium | History of mining, construction, shipbuilding |
| **Environmental** | Radon gas (second leading cause in non-smokers), air pollution (PM2.5) | Non-smoker with home in high-radon area or urban pollution |
| **Genetic/ familial** | First-degree relative with lung cancer (especially in never-smokers) | Young onset or non-smoker with family history |
| **Medical** | Chronic lung disease (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis – increased risk) | Patient already under respiratory follow-up |
| **Infectious** | Not a direct cause – but HIV (weakened immunity) increases risk | Immunosuppressed patient |
| **Other** | Prior thoracic radiotherapy (e.g., for breast cancer, lymphoma) | History of chest radiation |

**Most common cause:** Cigarette smoking – the dose-response is linear. Even second-hand smoke increases risk.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Histological confirmation from biopsy (CT-guided, bronchoscopic, EBUS-TBNA, or surgical) + staging imaging (PET-CT, MRI brain). Staging uses **TNM (8th edition)** – not detailed here, but crucial for management.

**First test for suspicion:**
- **Chest X-ray** – shows mass, effusion, collapse, hilar prominence. *However, normal CXR does not exclude cancer.*

**Critical work-up:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation trap |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **CT chest + upper abdomen** | Tumour size, location, invasion, mediastinal lymph nodes, liver/adrenal metastases | Definitive staging scan; needed for TNM | Don’t confuse atelectasis for tumour |
| **PET-CT** | Metabolic activity (SUV) | Identifies viable tumour vs scar; detects distant metastases | False positives in infection/inflammation |
| **MRI brain** | Brain metastases (lung cancer is #1 source of brain mets) | Mandatory for stage III–IV; consider for all NSCLC | Small nodules may be missed without contrast |
| **Bronchoscopy with biopsy** | Endobronchial tumour, washings, brushings | Central tumours; also for haemoptysis | Negative biopsy does not rule out – need peripheral needle |
| **CT-guided lung biopsy** | Peripheral nodule core | Diagnostic yield >90% | Pneumothorax risk (15-30%) |
| **EBUS-TBNA** | Mediastinal/hilar nodes | Nodal staging – crucial for deciding surgery vs chemoRT | False negatives in small nodes <5 mm |
| **Sputum cytology** | Malignant cells | Only useful for central, squamous or small cell – poor sensitivity | High false negative rate |

**Single finding that confirms:** Histopathology report showing malignant epithelial tumour with histological subtyping (adenocarcinoma, squamous, large cell) and molecular markers (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1). *You need both histology and molecular profile.*

**Pattern-recognition clue for subtype:** Cavitating central mass on CT = squamous cell; ground-glass nodule = lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma (formerly BAC).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

*Management is stage-driven – use multidisciplinary team (MDT) decision.*

**Immediate (first hour – after diagnosis):**
- **Stabilise if symptomatic:** Oxygen if hypoxic; analgesia for chest pain (avoid NSAIDs if renal impaired); treat post-obstructive pneumonia with antibiotics (co-amoxiclav IV).
- **Call respiratory oncology team** – don’t start chemotherapy without full staging.
- **If SVC syndrome:** Head-up position, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, urgent oncology review for radiotherapy/stenting.

**Ward (24-72 h – staging and planning):**
- **Confirm histological subtype and molecular profile.** Await EGFR/ALK/ROS1/PD-L1 results (5-10 days). Do NOT start empiric chemo before results – you may miss targeted therapy.
- **PET-CT and MRI brain** within 48 h if not done.
- **Pulmonary function tests** for surgical fitness (FEV1, DLCO) if early stage.
- **Consider VTE prophylaxis** (lung cancer + hypercoagulable) – enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily unless contraindicated.

**Long-term (MDT-defined plan):**

| Stage | Treatment | Why |
|-------|-----------|-----|
| **IA–IIB (T1-2 N0 M0)** | Lobectomy + mediastinal lymph node dissection (or segmentectomy for small GGO lesions) | Potentially curative; 5-yr survival 80-40% depending on stage |
| **IIIA (N2 positive)** | Neoadjuvant chemoRT (cisplatin + etoposide) then surgery, or definitive chemoRT | N2 disease requires combined modality |
| **IIIB–IIIC (unresectable)** | Definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy: cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1 + etoposide 100 mg/m² IV days 1-3, every 21 days, with RT 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions; then durvalumab (PD-L1 inhibitor) consolidation for 12 months | Durvalumab (PACIFIC regimen) improves PFS and OS |
| **IV (metastatic)** | If driver mutation +ve: targeted TKI (osimertinib for EGFR; alectinib for ALK; entrectinib for ROS1). If no driver: first-line chemo-immunotherapy (pembrolizumab + carboplatin + pemetrexed for non-squamous; for squamous: pembrolizumab + carboplatin + nab-paclitaxel). Cisplatin/pemetrexed also used. | Molecular testing guides first move |

**Drug doses (inline):**
- **Osimertinib** 80 mg PO once daily (first line EGFR mutant).
- **Alectinib** 600 mg PO twice daily (ALK rearrangement).
- **Pembrolizumab** 200 mg IV q3w (PD-L1 >50% monotherapy; or + chemotherapy if PD-L1 1-49%).
- **Carboplatin AUC5-6 IV q3w + pemetrexed** 500 mg/m² IV q3w (non-squamous).
- **Cisplatin** 75 mg/m² IV q3w.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ Don't give anti-EGFR TKI without EGFR mutation – will cause toxicity with no benefit.
- ❌ Don't start steroids for brain metastases before biopsy – may interfere with histology.
- ❌ Don't give prophylactic cranial irradiation in NSCLC (unlike SCLC).
- ❌ Don't offer surgery for N3 or M1 disease – it worsens survival.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | NSCLC | Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) | Pulmonary TB | Benign Lung Nodule (e.g., hamartoma) |
|---------|-------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Dull ache (chest wall invasion) | Usually none unless metastatic | Pleuritic chest pain if effusion | Usually none |
| **Exam** | Clubbing, reduced breath sounds | No clubbing; often SVC syndrome | Crepitations, may have tachypnoea | Normal |
| **CXR/CT** | Solitary nodule or mass; spiculated borders, cavitation (squamous), ground glass (adenocarcinoma) | Central mass, bulky mediastinal nodes, often early effusion | Apical infiltrates, cavitation (ghoul lesions), hilar nodes | Well-defined, round, sometimes “popcorn” calcification (cartilage) |
| **Labs** | Normal (unless paraneoplastic: hyperCa, ↑ALP) | Normal; can have SIADH, ACTH (Cushing’s) | Positive Quantiferon/IGRA, sputum AFB (if cavitary) | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Histology + molecular markers | Biopsy shows small cells with miliary necrosis; rapid doubling time (<30 days) | TB contacts, night sweats, fevers; positive culture/NAT | Stable on repeat CT (2 yr), non-FDG avid on PET |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

1. **Post-obstructive pneumonia:** Recurrent chest infections, slow resolution → lung abscess (foul sputum, high fever). **What to do:** CT chest + IV antibiotics (piperacillin/tazobactam), consider bronchoscopy for drainage.
2. **Malignant pleural effusion:** Dyspnoea, dullness, stony dull percussion. **Signs:** Shift of trachea away (if large). **What to do:** Ultrasound-guided thoracentesis (send for cytology, pH, glucose). Offer pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter.
3. **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO):** Facial fullness, distended neck veins, cyanosis, stridor. **Emergency.** **What to do:** Head-up, IV dexamethasone, repeat CT with contrast, urgent radiotherapy (8 Gy in 1 fraction for palliation) or SVC stent.
4. **Brain metastases:** Headache, nausea, seizures, focal deficits. **Signs:** Papilloedema on fundoscopy. **What to do:** MRI brain; IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat then 4 mg QDS; whole-brain RT or stereotactic radiosurgery if oligometastatic.
5. **Hypercalcaemia (squamous):** Lethargy, constipation, polyuria, confusion. ECG short QT. **What to do:** IV fluids 0.9% saline 1L over 1h then 3L/day, bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg IV). Check for PTHrP.
6. **Lung abscess (cavitating squamous):** High fever, productive cough, haemoptysis. CT shows cavity with air-fluid level. **What to do:** IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav + metronidazole), or percutaneous drainage if >3 cm.
7. **Pulmonary metastasis to the opposite lung:** New nodules on follow-up CT. This upsages to M1a.

**Late-presentation trap:** A patient with weight loss, back pain (vertebral metastasis), and leg weakness – may be misdiagnosed as spinal disc disease. *Always consider bone mets in a smoker with back pain.*

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **BEST Mnemonic for NSCLC workup:**  
  B – **Biopsy** (histology + molecular)  
  E – **Excision** (surgery if stage I-II)  
  S – **Staging** (PET-CT + MRI brain)  
  T – **Targeted therapy** (if driver mutation)

- **SQUAMOUS clues:**  
  S – Smoking, Sulcus (cavitation)  
  Q – Quit? No, they’re still smoking  
  U – Ugh, hypercalcaemia (PTHrP)  
  A – Atelectasis (central)  
  M – Mass in mediastinum (if big)  
  O – Oncology biopsy (PD-L1 high)  
  U – Ulceration? No  
  S – Spiculated margins

- **PARANEOPLASTIC (NSCLC specific):**  
  H – Hypercalcaemia (squamous)  
  O – Osteoarthropathy (HPOA, clubbing)  
  P – Peripheral neuropathy / LEMS  
  E – Endocrine (gynaecomastia – large cell)

**High-yield pearls:**

1. **Never clear a smoker’s “chest infection” without an X-ray** – if cough persists >6 weeks, CT is mandatory.
2. **Adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer in never-smokers and women.** Always test EGFR, ALK, ROS1 even if non-smoker.
3. **Clubbing + haemoptysis + weight loss = lung cancer until proven otherwise.**
4. **A single pulmonary nodule in a smoker >50 years old is malignant until proven otherwise** – need FDG-PET or biopsy.
5. **If you see a cavitating lesion in a smoker, think squamous cell carcinoma** – not always TB.
6. **Do not order a CT-guided biopsy for a small (5-10 mm) ground-glass nodule** – consider surveillance CT in 3 months (risk of pneumothorax outweighs benefit).
7. **When a patient with known NSCLC develops worsening dyspnoea, think malignant pericardial effusion, lymphangitic carcinomatosis, or pulmonary embolism** – all need urgent echo/CTPA.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- ❌ Ordering only chest X-ray to follow up a nodule – need CT for growth assessment.
- ❌ Assuming a non-smoker cannot have lung cancer.
- ❌ Starting steroids before biopsy when brain mets are suspected – risks missing diagnosis if lymphoma/toxoplasmosis.
- ❌ Giving prophylactic PCV vaccine (Pneumovax) before splenectomy – not relevant here, but sometimes confused with risk of post-obstructive pneumonia.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Trigger | When to call | What to do |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| **SVCO (urgent)** | Stridor, severe facial oedema, cyanosis, inability to lie flat | Call oncology/radiology for urgent stent or radiotherapy; ICU if airway compromise |
| **Massive haemoptysis** (>300 ml/24h or any with airway compromise) | Crash team, respiratory reg, thoracic surgery | Intubate if GCS<8, place patient bleeding side down, rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade |
| **NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4** | Sepsis secondary to post-obstructive pneumonia | ICU; start IV antibiotics, consider bronchoscopy for clearance |
| **GCS <13 or new focal neurology** | Suspect brain metastases or leptomeningeal disease | Urgent CT head, start dexamethasone 8 mg IV stat, call oncology |
| **Hypercalcaemia** (corrected Ca >3.0 mmol/L) | Medical reg for IV fluids + bisphosphonate; if symptomatic (ECG changes, confusion), call ICU | Cardiac monitoring, IV bisphosphonate, consider calcitonin |
| **Pleural effusion causing respiratory distress** | Respiratory reg for ultrasound-guided drainage | Diagnostic tap first (send for pH, protein, LDH, cytology); therapeutic tap if >1L until dyspnoea relieved |

**General threshold for ICU referral:** Any patient with rising NEWS2, persistent hypoxia (SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂), or need for non-invasive ventilation. Lung cancer patients can be very frail and may decline escalation – always have the goals of care discussion early.
